settings.title: Cedar deck & pergola
settings.summary: A rotting deck replaced with four hundred square feet of cedar, a timber pergola, and footings sized for the hot tub that's coming later.
settings.description:
The old deck was original to the house and past saving — undersized joists, rusted hangers, and posts sitting on soil. We demolished it in a day and started over from the footings.

Built from the footings up

New concrete footings below frost depth, structural screws instead of nails at every connection, and flashing tape over every joist: the parts you never see are the reason a deck stays flat and quiet for decades.

The new structure is framed to current code with a timber pergola over the dining end for summer shade. The stairs were widened and relanded so they put people onto the garden path instead of the lawn.

Sized for the someday hot tub

Because the owners mentioned a hot tub was on the someday list, we oversized the footings and framing in that corner now — a few hundred dollars during construction instead of a rebuild later.

The electrical conduit is already stubbed to that corner too. When the tub arrives, the work is a connection, not a construction project.

Three weeks, start to finish

Three weeks, including the permit. The cedar will silver out over a couple of seasons; the structure underneath is rated for decades.

We left the owners a one-page care sheet: what to wash, what to check each spring, and the one thing not to do, which is pressure-wash cedar at close range.
